title: JVM Low-Level Execution & GC Tuning
subtitle: Where your bytes really live, when the JIT wakes up, and what G1 vs ZGC costs you in microseconds
goal: Reason about a running JVM the way a latency engineer does: predict heap-vs-stack placement under escape analysis, compute exact object footprints, model G1 region and humongous allocation, estimate allocation rate and minor-GC frequency, and know when C1/C2 take over from the interpreter — then drive the same code under -XX:+UseG1GC, -XX:+UseZGC, -Xint, and -Xlog:gc to watch the prediction hold.

blocks.heading: Heap, stack, and the lie that 'objects always live on the heap'
blocks.html:
The textbook rule — primitives and references on the stack, objects on the heap — is the first thing a serious JVM engineer unlearns. The stack is per-thread: each method frame holds local primitives and object references, is allocated and freed in strict LIFO order, and never sees the garbage collector. The heap is shared across threads, is where new objects nominally land, and is the only region the GC scans.

But the JIT is allowed to break the rule when it can prove an object does not escape its creating method. If an object's reference never leaves the frame — never returned, never stored in a field, never handed to another thread — the compiler can scalar-replace it: shred the object into its individual fields and keep them in registers or on the stack. No heap allocation, no header, no GC pressure, zero deallocation cost. In a hot path that allocates millions of short-lived temporaries, this is the single biggest free win the JVM gives you.

blocks.heading: TLAB: why allocation is usually a pointer bump
blocks.html:
When an object does go on the heap, it almost never touches a global lock. Each thread owns a Thread-Local Allocation Buffer (TLAB) — a private slab carved out of Eden. Allocating is then just ptr += size; if (ptr > end) slowpath(): a bump-the-pointer with no synchronization. This is why Java allocation is often faster than a hand-rolled C malloc for small short-lived objects.

The slow path fires when the TLAB is exhausted: the thread either grabs a fresh TLAB or, for a large object, allocates directly in the shared heap (with a lock). The takeaway for HFT code: keep objects small and short-lived so they die in Eden, fit your TLAB, and never trigger the slow path on the hot path.

blocks.caption: Escape analysis in action — this allocation can vanish
blocks.explain: Three escape states matter: NoEscape (stack/scalar-replaceable), ArgEscape (passed to a callee but stays thread-local — lock elision possible), and GlobalEscape (stored to a static/field or published to another thread — must live on the heap). Only NoEscape objects get scalar-replaced. Toggle -XX:+PrintEscapeAnalysis (fastdebug builds) or just compare throughput with -XX:+DoEscapeAnalysis vs -XX:-DoEscapeAnalysis.

blocks.heading: Object footprint: header + fields + alignment padding
blocks.html:
Every heap object carries an object header. On a 64-bit HotSpot with compressed oops (the default below 32 GB heaps), the header is 12 bytes: an 8-byte mark word (identity hash, lock state, GC age bits) plus a 4-byte compressed klass pointer. Arrays add a 4-byte length, making their header 16 bytes.

Field widths: boolean/byte = 1, char/short = 2, int/float = 4, long/double = 8, and a reference = 4 bytes under compressed oops. The total instance size is then padded up to a multiple of 8 bytes (the object alignment). That padding is real wasted memory: a class with a single boolean still costs 16 bytes (12 header + 1 field + 3 padding).

Compressed oops are why you keep your max heap under the ~32 GB boundary if you can: cross it and references widen to 8 bytes, every object grows, and your effective cache footprint shrinks — a heap that was comfortable at 31 GB can thrash at 33 GB. The JVM logs Compressed Oops mode at startup; check it with -Xlog:gc+heap+coops=info.

blocks.heading: G1: the heap is a grid of regions, and big objects are 'humongous'
blocks.html:
G1 (Garbage First), the default collector since JDK 9, abandons the old contiguous young/old split. It carves the heap into ~2048 equal-size regions (region size is a power of two from 1 MB to 32 MB, auto-chosen from heap size). A region is dynamically tagged Eden, Survivor, or Old. G1 collects the regions with the most garbage first — hence the name — to hit a soft pause-time target (-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis, default 200 ms).

The trap is the humongous object: any single allocation ≥ 50% of one region is allocated directly in Old across ceil(size / regionSize) contiguous regions, bypassing Eden entirely. Humongous objects fragment the heap, are expensive to collect, and can force full GCs if contiguous regions can't be found. A 9 MB array in a JVM with 8 MB regions burns two whole regions and wastes 7 MB. Size your regions (or your objects) so hot allocations stay normal.

blocks.caption: Selecting and observing G1
blocks.explain: Read GC logs as before->after(committed): the drop is how much you reclaimed, the parenthesized number is total committed heap, the trailing ms is the stop-the-world pause. Frequent 'Pause Young' with small drops means a high allocation rate of short-lived garbage; any 'Pause Full' under G1 usually means humongous fragmentation or an undersized heap.

blocks.heading: ZGC: when you trade throughput for sub-millisecond pauses
blocks.html:
ZGC (-XX:+UseZGC) is a concurrent, region-based collector built for pause times under 1 ms regardless of heap size — it scales from a few hundred MB to multiple terabytes with pauses that stay flat. It does almost all its work (marking and relocation) concurrently with your application, using colored pointers and load barriers: a few extra instructions on every reference load that let the collector move objects out from under running threads.

The trade-off is real. ZGC's load barriers and concurrent relocation cost throughput — typically a few percent versus G1 — and its memory overhead is higher. Choose G1 when you want balanced throughput and acceptable (tens of ms) pauses; choose ZGC when a single multi-ms stop-the-world pause would blow your latency SLA — a market-data handler that cannot afford a 30 ms G1 pause during a volatility spike. Run the same workload under -XX:+UseG1GC -Xlog:gc and -XX:+UseZGC -Xlog:gc and compare the pause column; the correctness output is identical, the pause profile is not.

ZGC pauses being tiny does not mean GC is free. The concurrent work still burns CPU and the load barriers tax every reference read, so a throughput-bound batch job can run measurably slower under ZGC than G1 even though its pauses are shorter. 'Low pause' and 'high throughput' are different objectives — pick the one your workload is actually graded on.

blocks.heading: Allocation rate and GC pressure: the real driver of pause frequency
blocks.html:
Pause frequency is governed by one number: allocation rate (bytes/sec of new objects). Every byte you allocate fills Eden; when Eden fills, a minor GC fires to evacuate the survivors and reset Eden. So minor-GC frequency ≈ allocationRate / edenSize. Double your allocation rate and you double your minor GCs; double Eden and you halve them (at the cost of longer individual pauses and more memory).

This is why allocation reduction beats GC tuning in the hot path. Object pooling, primitive arrays instead of boxed Integer, reusing buffers, and letting escape analysis kill temporaries all cut the allocation rate at the source. The cheapest GC is the one that never has to run because Eden never filled. Most short-lived objects (the weak generational hypothesis) die in Eden and cost almost nothing to collect — the danger is the few that survive long enough to get promoted to Old, where collection is expensive.

blocks.heading: JIT: the interpreter, C1, and C2
blocks.html:
Java starts interpreted — the JVM walks bytecode directly, slow but instantly available. As a method runs, HotSpot increments per-method invocation and back-edge counters. Cross a threshold and tiered compilation kicks in: the C1 (client) compiler produces fast-to-emit, lightly optimized, profiled machine code; once a method is proven genuinely hot, C2 (server) recompiles it with aggressive optimizations — inlining, escape analysis, loop unrolling, vectorization — using the profile C1 gathered.

The practical consequences: (1) warm-up is real — the first thousands of calls run interpreted or under C1, so a freshly started process is slow until C2 catches up; HFT systems warm the JIT with synthetic traffic before going live. (2) -Xint forces pure interpretation (no JIT) — useful to measure the JIT's contribution, brutally slow in production. (3) C2 can deoptimize: if a speculative assumption (e.g., a class was never subclassed) is violated, it bails back to the interpreter and recompiles. Benchmark only after warm-up, or your numbers measure the interpreter, not your code.

blocks.caption: Observing the tiers
blocks.explain: The number column in -XX:+PrintCompilation is the tier: 0 interpreter, 1-3 are C1 variants (3 = profiled C1), 4 = C2. 'made not entrant' / 'made zombie' are deoptimizations. If a method you expect to be hot never reaches tier 4, it may be too big to inline or its profile is unstable — both kill peak performance.

blocks.question: A method creates a temporary object that it only reads from and never returns, stores, or shares with another thread. Under C2 with escape analysis enabled, what most likely happens to that object?
options: It is always allocated on the heap and collected by the next minor GC.
It is scalar-replaced — its fields live in registers/stack and no heap object is allocated at all.
It is moved to the Old generation immediately to avoid GC.
It forces a humongous allocation because temporaries are large.
blocks.explain: An object that does not escape its method (NoEscape) is a scalar-replacement candidate: C2 shreds it into its individual fields, keeps them in registers or on the stack, and skips heap allocation entirely — no header, no GC, no deallocation. This is the biggest free win in allocation-heavy hot paths.

blocks.question: You have a market-data handler where a single 30 ms stop-the-world GC pause during a volatility burst would violate your latency SLA, even though total throughput matters less. Which collector fits best, and why?
options: G1, because it has the highest raw throughput.
-Xint, because no JIT means predictable timing.
ZGC, because its concurrent relocation and load barriers keep pauses under ~1 ms regardless of heap size, trading some throughput for pause predictability.
The Serial collector, because single-threaded GC is the most predictable.
blocks.explain: ZGC does marking and relocation concurrently using colored pointers and load barriers, holding pauses below ~1 ms even on large heaps. You pay a few percent throughput and higher memory overhead, but you eliminate the multi-ms stop-the-world pause that would breach the SLA — exactly the trade an HFT latency engineer wants.

blocks.title: Compute an object's footprint by hand
blocks.instructions: A class on 64-bit HotSpot with compressed oops has one int field and one reference field. Header is 12 bytes, int is 4, a compressed reference is 4, and the total is padded up to a multiple of 8. Print the total instance size in bytes.
blocks.passMsg: Correct — 12 + 8 = 20, padded up to 24. The 4 padding bytes are real wasted memory.
blocks.failMsg: Total = roundUpTo8(header + fields) = roundUpTo8(12 + 8) = roundUpTo8(20) = 24. Use (raw + 7) & ~7.

problems.title: Escape-analysis allocator: stack vs heap split
problems.instructions:
Model what escape analysis does to a batch of allocations. Line 1: an integer q (1 ≤ q ≤ 100000), the number of allocation groups. Each of the next q lines is <escape> <size> <count> where escape is one of NONE, ARG, GLOBAL; size is the per-object byte size (1 ≤ size ≤ 10^6); count is how many such objects are allocated (1 ≤ count ≤ 10^6).

Rule: only NONE (does-not-escape) objects are scalar-replaced and counted as stack allocations; ARG and GLOBAL escape and count as heap allocations. For each class, accumulate the object count and the total bytes (size × count, may exceed 32 bits — use long).

Output exactly two lines:
stack <stackObjects> <stackBytes>
heap <heapObjects> <heapBytes>

The graded answer is the deterministic byte/object split. Use the ▶ Run and ⏱ Profile buttons with the attached flags to watch how toggling escape analysis changes real GC behavior.

tests.name: hidden: nothing escapes, large byte total

problems.hint: Only escape==NONE is scalar-replaced (stack). ARG and GLOBAL both escape, so both go to heap. bytes = size*count and can exceed Integer.MAX_VALUE — accumulate in long.

problems.title: Object footprint calculator (compressed oops)
problems.instructions:
Compute the exact heap footprint of one instance on 64-bit HotSpot with compressed oops. Line 1: integer f (0 ≤ f ≤ 1000), the number of fields. Each of the next f lines is a field type, one of: boolean, byte, char, short, int, float, long, double, ref.

Widths (bytes): boolean=1, byte=1, char=2, short=2, int=4, float=4, long=8, double=8, ref=4 (compressed oop). The object header is 12 bytes. Sum header + all field widths, then pad the total up to a multiple of 8 bytes. (For this model, sum field widths directly — do not reorder or insert inter-field gaps; only the final 8-byte alignment padding applies.)

Output exactly four lines:
header 12
fields <sumOfFieldWidths>
padding <bytesAddedForAlignment>
total <finalSizePaddedTo8>
problems.hint: header=12, ref=4 under compressed oops. raw = 12 + sum(widths); total = (raw + 7) & ~7; padding = total - raw. An object with no fields is 12 -> padded to 16.

problems.title: G1 region tracker with humongous detection
problems.instructions:
Simulate how G1 places allocations into fixed-size regions. Line 1: integer regionKb — the region size in KiB (a power of two, 1024 ≤ regionKb ≤ 32768). Line 2: integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 200000), the number of allocations. The next n lines each give one object's size in bytes (1 ≤ size ≤ 2×10^9).

Let regionBytes = regionKb × 1024. An object is humongous when size ≥ 50% of a region (i.e. size × 2 ≥ regionBytes); it consumes ceil(size / regionBytes) dedicated regions. Normal objects are packed greedily into regions in input order: keep filling the current region while the running used bytes plus the new object stay ≤ regionBytes; otherwise open a new region. A normal object is never split across regions.

Output exactly three lines:
humongous <humongousObjs> <humongousRegions>
normal <normalObjs> <normalRegions>
regions <humongousRegions + normalRegions>

tests.name: visible: two normals pack, two humongous
hidden: four normals pack into two regions
hidden: object exactly one region is humongous

problems.hint: regionBytes = regionKb*1024. Humongous iff size*2 >= regionBytes, costing ceil(size/regionBytes) regions. Pack normals greedily: open a new region whenever usedInCurrent + size > regionBytes. Use long everywhere — sizes reach 2e9.

problems.title: Allocation-rate minor-GC counter
problems.instructions:
Estimate minor-GC frequency from an allocation stream against a fixed Eden. Line 1: integer edenKb — Eden capacity in KiB (1 ≤ edenKb ≤ 10^6). Line 2: integer g (1 ≤ g ≤ 100000), the number of allocation groups. Each of the next g lines is <size> <count>: allocate count objects (1 ≤ count ≤ 10^6) of size bytes each (1 ≤ size ≤ edenKb×1024, so any single object fits in empty Eden).

Model: maintain used bytes in Eden, starting at 0. To allocate one object of size: if used + size > edenBytes, a minor GC fires first (reset used to 0, increment the GC counter), then place the object (used += size). Process objects in input order, expanding each group's count. Accumulate total bytes allocated.

Output exactly two lines:
allocated <totalBytesAllocated>
minorGC <numberOfMinorGCs>

Run with -Xlog:gc attached and watch the real minor-GC count track your prediction as allocation rate climbs.

tests.name: hidden: object exactly fills Eden each time

problems.hint: GC fires BEFORE placing the object that wouldn't fit: if used+size>edenBytes then used=0 and count++. minor-GC frequency tracks allocationRate/edenSize — that's the whole point of the model.

problems.title: JIT tier promotion simulator (C1/C2)
problems.instructions:
Model HotSpot tiered compilation with simplified thresholds: C1_THRESHOLD = 2000 and C2_THRESHOLD = 10000 cumulative invocations. Line 1: integer q (1 ≤ q ≤ 200000), the number of invocation events. Each of the next q lines is METHOD <name> <invocations> (name has no spaces; 1 ≤ invocations ≤ 10^7). Events for the same method accumulate.

After summing all events, each method's compilation level is decided by its cumulative invocation count: < 2000 → INTERPRETED; 2000 ≤ c < 10000 → C1; c ≥ 10000 → C2.

Output one line per method in first-seen order:
<name> <cumulativeCount> <LEVEL>
then a final line c2 <numberOfMethodsAtC2>.

Run under -Xint (pure interpreter, no JIT) versus the default and Profile both to feel exactly how much throughput C2 buys you.
problems.stdin:
4
METHOD hot 1500
METHOD cold 50
METHOD hot 600
METHOD veryhot 12000

tests.name: visible: mixed tiers, accumulation
tests.stdin:
4
METHOD hot 1500
METHOD cold 50
METHOD hot 600
METHOD veryhot 12000
tests.expectStdout:
hot 2100 C1
cold 50 INTERPRETED
veryhot 12000 C2
c2 1

problems.hint: Use a LinkedHashMap<String,Long> so methods print in first-seen order; merge with Long::sum to accumulate repeated events. Level boundaries are inclusive at the low end: exactly 2000 is C1, exactly 10000 is C2.
